text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
Trial Response Assessment (Cycle 4 Timepoint / Approximately 8 Weeks on Study)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
64-year-old female with known stage IV left lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, currently enrolled on Phase I/II investigational clinical trial (EGFR inhibitor plus PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor combination). Prior treatment history includes osimertinib (first-line, discontinued after approximately 14 months due to acquired T790M resistance and systemic/intracranial progression), stereotactic radiosurgery to three brain metastases (right frontal, right parietal, left cerebellar), carboplatin/pemetrexed induction chemotherapy (4 cycles), and pemetrexed maintenance (3 cycles, discontinued at second systemic progression). Scan obtained for protocol-mandated oncologic response assessment following 2 cycles of investigational combination therapy. Comparison is made to the most recent prior CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis obtained at the time of second systemic progression (pre-trial enrollment baseline).

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast material via right antecubital vein at a rate of 3 mL/sec. Imaging was obtained during the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis, and during the arterial phase for the chest. Reconstructions were performed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes with 2.5 mm slice thickness. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and bone windows were reviewed. Oral contrast was not administered. The examination was technically adequate.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The central airways are patent. The trachea maintains a midline course. Both main bronchi are patent without endobronchial lesion.

The dominant left upper lobe lesion centered in the posterior segment is again identified. On the current examination, this mass has decreased in size compared with the most recent prior study on which it measured 4.0 cm in greatest axial dimension. It currently measures approximately 3.0 cm x 2.7 cm (previously 4.0 cm x 3.4 cm), representing a meaningful interval decrease. The lesion retains a somewhat irregular, spiculated morphology with associated pleural tethering along the left costal pleural surface. There is persistent mild interstitial thickening in the immediately adjacent lung parenchyma that likely reflects postobstructive or inflammatory change; this appears similar to the prior study. No new pulmonary nodules or satellite lesions are identified in either lung. The remaining left lung parenchyma appears clear. The right lung fields are clear without consolidation, effusion, or new nodular density.

There has been near-complete resolution of the previously noted small right-sided pleural effusion. Trace bilateral pleural thickening is present, likely chronic and unchanged. No new pleural effusion is identified on either side. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The mediastinum is not widened. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. No new mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy identified. Previously described mediastinal lymph nodes at stations 4L, 5, and 6 continue to appear small and are not convincingly enlarged on current examination, consistent with prior response to osimertinib and subsequent treatment. A subcentimeter node in the left hilar region is stable and not clearly pathologic in size. The right hilum is unremarkable.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified in the ribs, sternum, clavicles, or visualized thoracic vertebral bodies. No pathologic fractures.

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and contour. The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous on arterial and portal venous phase imaging. A previously described hypodense lesion in hepatic segment VI, which measured 0.9 cm at the pre-trial baseline scan, has decreased in conspicuity and now measures approximately 0.6 cm in greatest dimension. This lesion enhances mildly during the portal venous phase and has become less well-defined compared with prior, raising the possibility of ongoing treatment response. No new hepatic lesions are identified in any segment. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein and hepatic veins appear patent on this examination.

Gallbladder and Biliary System:
The gallbladder is unremarkable without stones or wall thickening. The common bile duct is normal in caliber.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size at approximately 9.8 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesion. No splenomegaly.

Pancreas:
The pancreas enhances homogeneously without focal mass, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic stranding. The pancreatic tail is visualized to its entirety.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands:
The kidneys are symmetric in size and morphology. There is normal bilateral cortical enhancement. A few small bilateral cortical cysts are noted, unchanged and of no clinical significance.

The right adrenal gland again demonstrates an oval soft tissue nodule measuring approximately 2.0 cm in greatest dimension (previously 2.0 cm at baseline pre-trial scan, and 1.1 cm at initial discovery, growing to 2.0 cm at the time of second progression). On the current examination, this nodule appears essentially unchanged in size and morphology compared with the most recent prior study, with enhancement characteristics that remain consistent with metastatic disease given this patient's history. This lesion is stable.

The left adrenal gland appears normal without nodule or mass.

Other Abdominal Structures:
No ascites. No enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes. The stomach, small bowel, and visible segments of large bowel appear unremarkable. No pneumoperitoneum.

Vascular structures: The abdominal aorta and its major branches are normal in caliber. No aneurysmal dilatation. The inferior vena cava is normal.

PELVIS:
The urinary bladder is partially filled and smooth-walled. The uterus appears small and atrophic, consistent with postmenopausal status. No adnexal masses. No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. No pelvic free fluid. The sigmoid colon and rectum are unremarkable.

BONE WINDOWS:
No new suspicious osseous lesions in the imaged skeleton, including the pelvis, lumbar vertebrae, sacrum, and proximal femora. No pathologic fractures. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine are noted, consistent with age.

IMPRESSION:

1. INTERVAL DECREASE in the dominant left upper lobe primary lung mass, now measuring approximately 3.0 cm compared with 4.0 cm on the most recent prior examination, consistent with meaningful tumor response in the setting of investigational combination therapy.

2. INTERVAL DECREASE in the hepatic segment VI lesion, now measuring approximately 0.6 cm compared with 0.9 cm at baseline pre-trial scan, further supporting a favorable response to current treatment.

3. STABLE right adrenal metastasis, measuring 2.0 cm, unchanged compared with the prior study.

4. NEAR-COMPLETE RESOLUTION of the previously described small right-sided pleural effusion.

5. No new pulmonary nodules, new hepatic lesions, new lymphadenopathy, or new osseous lesions to suggest interval development of additional metastatic sites.

6. Stable mediastinal and hilar lymph node configuration; no convincing new adenopathy.

Overall, the findings are consistent with a favorable tumor response to the current investigational regimen, with reduction in the primary pulmonary lesion and the hepatic metastasis, stability of the adrenal metastasis, and resolution of the pleural effusion. Clinical and protocol correlation is recommended.

Reviewing Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist Signature]
